text.en: Who is speaking these words, and in what city is he king?
The Teacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem (v1).
In verse 5, name in order the three things the sun does.
It rises, then it sets, then it hurries back to where it rises (v5): “The sun rises and the sun sets; it hurries back to where it rises.”
Draw one picture of the sun coming up and one of the sun going down, the way verse 5 says. Then point to which one happens first.
Either order can start the picture, but per v5 the stated order is the sun rises, then sets, then hurries back to rise again. Affirm the child’s drawing and re-read v5 together.
The Teacher opens with “Futility of futilities.” What is he saying about everything?
That everything is futile (v2). The doubled form “futility of futilities” is the passage’s way of saying the very utmost, most complete futility — the repetition intensifies the meaning.
The phrase “under the sun” appears in verses 3 and 9. What does the Teacher mean by it?

This present life in this present world — things as they are here below, considered on their own. It is a phrase peculiar to Ecclesiastes; keep the sense to ‘this life under heaven’ rather than importing outside definitions (vv3, 9).
Verse 3 asks, “What does a man gain from all his labor, at which he toils under the sun?” What does “gain” mean here, and how does the rest of the passage answer the question?

“Gain” is real, lasting profit — a benefit that remains. The passage answers by observation: generations pass, sun and wind and rivers only return where they began, “there is nothing new under the sun” (v9), and even remembrance fails (v11). So labor under the sun, taken by itself, yields no abiding gain. Anchor the answer in what the verses observe; do not speculate beyond them.
“Futility of futilities,” says the Teacher, “futility of futilities! Everything is futile!” (Ecclesiastes 1:2)
What has been will be again, and what has been done will be done again; there is nothing new under the sun. (Ecclesiastes 1:9)
Finish the Teacher’s line from verse 9: there is nothing new under the ____.
“sun” — “there is nothing new under the sun” (v9).
As the passage is read aloud, listen for the phrase “under the sun.” Raise your hand each time you hear it.
“under the sun” occurs in v3 and v9 — two times in this pericope.
As verse 2 is read aloud, listen for the word the Teacher repeats to describe everything, and count how many times you hear it.
futility / futile — in v2 the root sounds five times: “Futility of futilities,” says the Teacher, “futility of futilities! Everything is futile!” (futility, futilities, futility, futilities, futile).
Verse 9 says “What has been will be again.” Later in Ecclesiastes, one verse ties this same returning cycle to God’s own ordering. Which verse is it?

Ecclesiastes 3:15 — “What exists has already been, and what will be has already been” — sets the cycle of 1:9 within God’s ordering. Name that reference; the point of contact is the recurring “what has been”.
Verse 4 says generations come and go “but the earth remains forever.” Which psalm also speaks of God fixing the earth on its foundations so that it endures?
Psalm 104:5 — God set the earth on its foundations so that it should not be moved. It matches Ecclesiastes 1:4’s picture of the earth abiding while generations pass. Name Psalm 104:5.
The Teacher says everything is “futile.” Write down one question you would want to ask him about that.

Keep this fully open — the aim is the child’s own wondering, not a target answer. Welcome any honest question and, if helpful, invite them to look back at the verses that prompted it. Do not steer toward a ‘right’ question or supply the answer yourself.
The Teacher keeps saying “under the sun.” What question does that repeated phrase raise for you?

Leave this open. Many learners will wonder what lies beyond ‘under the sun,’ but let the question be theirs — don’t pre-load the answer. If they ask, point them back to how the phrase is used in vv3 and 9 and let them keep wondering.

Verses 5–7 describe the sun, the wind, and the rivers, and each keeps returning to where it began. Reading these alongside verse 3 (“What does a man gain…”) and verse 8, what do these tireless-yet-returning motions picture about human labor?

First fix what the text states: sun rises and sets and hurries back (v5), wind swirls round and returns on its course (v6), rivers run to the sea yet the sea is never full and the streams flow again (v7). Then read them as figures for human toil — endless, circular striving that never arrives, which is why v8 turns straight back to man: “All things are wearisome, more than one can describe; the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear content with hearing.” Keep the interpretation tied to these verses; don’t import a moral the text doesn’t state.
Verse 8 says “the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear content with hearing.” Why might the Teacher place this right after the rivers that flow yet never fill the sea (v7)?

Anchor first in what v7 and v8 say: the sea is never full though all rivers pour in; the eye is never satisfied though it keeps seeing. The nearness suggests the same pattern moving from nature to the person — restless taking-in that never reaches ‘full.’ Let learners state the link from the text; affirm readings grounded in vv7–8 rather than a single required wording.
In your own words, retell verses 1–11: who is speaking, the one question he asks about labor, the things in nature he points to, and what he concludes about anything being new.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the text rather than grading it to a script. Together, see whether it touched: the speaker, the Teacher/son of David/king in Jerusalem (v1); his thesis that all is futile (v2); the question “What does a man gain from all his labor, at which he toils under the sun?” (v3); generations passing while the earth remains (v4); the sun, wind, and rivers that keep returning (vv5–7); weariness and the unsatisfied eye and ear (v8); and “nothing new under the sun,” with remembrance failing (vv9–11). If a part was missing, have them re-read it and add it — don’t supply a model retelling.

Over the next day, keep a short written list of tasks you do that you also did yesterday. Bring the list, and beside it write one sentence on how verse 9 (“there is nothing new under the sun”) reads against your own week.

This asks for an observable action — a written list and one written sentence — not a self-assessment. Receive whatever is brought; do not push toward gloom or grade the reflection. The point is simply to notice the passage’s theme of recurring labor in one’s own ordinary week, staying with what v9 actually says.
What does the speaker call himself, and over which nation and city was he king?
He calls himself the Teacher, and he was king over Israel in Jerusalem (v12).
As you listen, catch how the speaker names himself and the land and city he ruled.
The Teacher, who was king over Israel in Jerusalem (v12).
The Teacher twice calls what he found "a pursuit of the wind." What does that image say about what he chased?
Wind cannot be caught or held; the image says his findings proved empty and could not be grasped or kept, however hard he searched (v14, v17).
The Teacher searches all that is done "under heaven" and "under the sun." What sphere of things do these repeated phrases mark out?
The whole realm of human works and life in this present world, which he set his mind to seek and explore (v13-14).
Listen for the same short phrase the Teacher uses again and again to describe what he found.
"A pursuit of the wind," said in both v14 and v17.
For with much wisdom comes much sorrow, and as knowledge grows, grief increases. (Ecclesiastes 1:18)
Fill in the Teacher's words from verse 15 — What is crooked cannot be ______, and what is lacking cannot be ______.
"straightened" and "counted" (v15).
The Teacher found that more wisdom brought more sorrow. According to Job 28:28, named in the study notes, what is the true wisdom his search under the sun kept missing?
Job 28:28 declares that the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom, and to turn away from evil is understanding.

In verse 14 the Teacher looks at all things done under the sun and finds them futile. When he surveys his own accomplishments again in Ecclesiastes 2:11 (see the study notes), what verdict does he reach there?
The same verdict: Ecclesiastes 2:11 says he found everything to be futile, a pursuit of the wind, with nothing to be gained under the sun.
The Teacher says that as his knowledge grew, so did his grief. What questions does that stir up in you about the things you most want to learn?

Let learners voice their own wondering; do not supply a verdict. Notice questions raised, without judging the faith behind them. Honest wonderings might touch why learning can bring pain, or where lasting satisfaction is found.
As you listen, write down one honest question you would want to ask the Teacher about his search and what he found.
This prompt gathers the learner's own question, not a right answer; receive whatever question is written and note it as evidence of engagement, not as a measure of the person.
The Teacher had grown "in wisdom beyond all those before" him, yet still calls his search futile. From the passage itself, why could even his great wisdom not satisfy him or mend the world?

Anchor answers in the text: his wisdom could not straighten what is crooked nor supply what is lacking (v15), and the more he knew, the more his sorrow and grief increased (v18). Human wisdom, excellent in its place, cannot by itself satisfy the soul or reform a fallen world; that is why he names it a burden and a pursuit of the wind.
Retell this passage in your own words, as if you were the Teacher: what you set out to do, what you found, and what you concluded about it.

A good retelling includes the setup (a king in Jerusalem setting his mind to seek out everything by wisdom), the finding (all of it futile, a pursuit of the wind, unable to straighten the crooked), and the conclusion (more wisdom brought more sorrow). Listen for the shape of the argument, not exact words.
Name one specific matter this week that you will bring to God in prayer instead of trying to solve it by your own cleverness alone, and write down when you will do it.

Look for a concrete, doable action (a named matter and a set time), not a statement about the person's faith. Record only what they plan to do; the passage's point is that human wisdom alone cannot straighten the crooked, so turning a matter to God is a fitting, observable response.

Draw a picture of someone trying to catch the wind with their hands. Then talk about it: the Teacher searched and searched, and said everything he found was like chasing the wind, because you cannot hold it. (For older children: make two columns on paper, one for what the Teacher studied and one for the words he used for what he found, "a pursuit of the wind.")

Wind cannot be caught, so the picture shows why the Teacher's search felt empty. For the older-child columns: what he studied includes wisdom, knowledge, and madness and folly (v13, v16, v17); what he found is "a pursuit of the wind" (v14, v17).
In which city did the writer say he became greater than everyone who had lived before him?
Jerusalem — he surpassed all in Jerusalem who had preceded him (v9; also v7).
The writer built and planted many things for himself. Name one thing he built or planted.
Any one of: houses, vineyards (v4); gardens, parks, fruit trees (v5); reservoirs / groves of trees (v6).
Draw one thing the writer made in the passage: a house, a vineyard, a garden, or a pool of water for the trees.
All four appear in the text: houses and vineyards (v4), gardens/parks (v5), and reservoirs to water the groves (v6). Any faithful drawing is right; help pre-readers name what they drew.
In this passage, which comes first: the writer building his houses and gardens, or his conclusion that everything was futile?
The building comes first — he lists all he built and gathered (v4-10), and only at the end, on review, delivers the verdict: “Yet when I considered all the works...” (v11).
The writer keeps calling his results “futile.” From how he uses the word here, what does he mean his pleasures and works failed to give him?
Not that the things were evil, but that they yielded no solid, lasting satisfaction — “nothing to be gained under the sun” (v11). Sought as the highest good, created goods leave the soul empty.
The writer weighs what is worthwhile “under heaven” and finds nothing gained “under the sun.” What horizon of life do those phrases point to?
This-worldly life and labor — what men do “under heaven during the few days of their lives” (v3), the earthly frame within which he ran his test (v11).
The writer says all his works were “a pursuit of the wind.” What is it like to chase after the wind?
You can run after wind but never catch it or hold it — you end with nothing in your hands. That is his picture of works that leave “nothing to be gained” (v11).

“Yet when I considered all the works that my hands had accomplished and what I had toiled to achieve, I found everything to be futile, a pursuit of the wind; there was nothing to be gained under the sun.” (Ecclesiastes 2:11)
I said to myself, “Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy what is good!” But it proved to be futile. (Ecclesiastes 2:1)
Finish the writer's words from verse 1: Come now, I will test you with ___.
“pleasure” — “Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy what is good!” (v1).
Verse 11 ends “futile, a pursuit of the wind.” This is the book's recurring refrain. Where earlier in Ecclesiastes 1 is that same verdict first stated?
Ecclesiastes 1:14 — there he first says he “found them all to be futile, a pursuit of the wind” (named in the brief as the book's refrain). Verse 2:11 repeats it after the pleasure experiment.

In verse 1 the writer talks to himself: “Come now, I will test you with pleasure; enjoy what is good!” A rich man in one of Jesus' parables speaks the same way to his own soul. Which parable, and where?

The Parable of the Rich Fool, Luke 12:19 (named in the brief), where the man tells his soul to take it easy and eat, drink, and be merry. Same self-address to pleasure, and God calls him a fool that night.
The writer gave his eyes and heart every desire (v10) yet gained nothing lasting. Which New Testament letter says the lust of the eyes and flesh and the pride of life are passing away?
1 John 2:16-17 (named in the brief) — the desire of the flesh, the desire of the eyes, and the pride of life pass away, but whoever does God's will abides forever.
The writer had anything his eyes wanted, yet still was not truly happy. What do you wonder about when you read that?

Keep this open — you are drawing out the child's own wondering, not steering to a set answer. Welcome any honest question (Why wasn't he happy? What would make him happy? Is wanting things bad?). Write the questions down; you don't need to resolve them here.
The writer ran a deliberate experiment on pleasure and reported the result. What question would you most want to put to him after reading his conclusion in verse 11?

This invites the learner's own question — do not supply one. Note where each person's question lands (the method of the test, the honesty of the verdict, what he thinks IS worthwhile) and let those questions steer the family's later reading rather than closing them off.
According to verse 11, when the writer reviewed everything his hands had done, what did he say was “gained under the sun”? Why do you think all that pleasure and work still left him empty?

Anchor first in the text: he says there was “nothing to be gained under the sun” (v11). Then let them reason from what the passage shows — every good was sought “for myself” (v4-8) and weighed only “under the sun,” so created things, made the highest good, cannot fill the soul. Don't push past what the text plainly says.

Even while testing wine and pleasure, the writer notes his “mind still guiding me with wisdom” (v3) and that “my wisdom remained with me” (v9). Why does his staying clear-headed make his final verdict more trustworthy?

The text twice insists he kept his wisdom (v3, v9): this was a controlled trial, not a drunkard's slide. He stayed master of his pleasures rather than their slave, so he is a competent, sober judge of them — which is why his “nothing to be gained” (v11) carries weight. Keep the reasoning tied to those verses.
In your own words, retell the whole passage (verses 1-11): what the writer set out to test, the things he built and gathered, and what he concluded when he looked back over all of it.

Keep this open — check the retelling against the text, don't grade it against a script. Look together for: the plan to test pleasure (v1-3); the catalog of works, wealth, and delights (v4-10); and the closing verdict that it was all futile, with nothing gained (v11). If a part is missing, re-read it and let them add it.
On an index card, write or draw one thing you spend a lot of time or effort chasing after this week. Underneath, copy what verse 11 says was “gained under the sun.”

This asks for a concrete, observable action, not a judgment of anyone's heart. The line they copy is “nothing to be gained under the sun” (v11). Let the card prompt reflection; do not turn it into an assessment of whether the child's pursuit is good or bad.
Listen for all the different things the writer builds, makes, or gathers for himself.

Houses, vineyards (v4); gardens, parks, fruit trees (v5); reservoirs and groves (v6); menservants, maidservants, herds and flocks (v7); silver, gold, treasure, singers (v8). Any several they catch is a good listen.
Listen for the word “futile” and the phrase “pursuit of the wind” — where do they fall in the passage?
“Futile” frames the whole test: it opens the experiment (v1) and closes the verdict (v11), where it pairs with “a pursuit of the wind.” The word bookends everything in between.
Listen for the moment the writer turns from listing his works to judging them, and note one question that turn raises for you.

The turn is “Yet when I considered all the works...” (v11), where enumeration becomes retrospective judgment. This listen-for asks for the learner's own question, so welcome whatever it surfaces rather than supplying one.
A central contrast in this passage is between the wise man and the fool. According to verses 13-14, what does the Preacher say sets them apart?
He distinguishes them by sight and darkness: 'The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness' (v.14), because 'wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness' (v.13).

verse.en: The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also came to realize that one fate overcomes them both.

text.en: In the closing verses (24-26), who does the passage name as the one who gives the good of enjoyment, and to whom does He give it?
God is the giver: the enjoyment of food, drink, and work is 'from the hand of God' (v.24), and 'To the man who is pleasing in His sight, He gives wisdom and knowledge and joy' (v.26).

verse.en: To the man who is pleasing in His sight, He gives wisdom and knowledge and joy, but to the sinner He assigns the task of gathering and accumulating that which he will hand over to one who pleases God.

text.en: As the passage is read, listen for the key contrast the Preacher draws between the one who sees and the one who walks in the dark.
The contrast is between the wise man and the fool (vv.13-14): 'The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness' (v.14), yet 'one fate overcomes them both' (v.14).

The Preacher's thought moves in steps. Put these in the order the passage gives them: he hated life; he turned to consider wisdom and folly; he saw that wisdom exceeds folly; he concluded that nothing is better than to enjoy one's work as from God's hand; he realized one fate overcomes the wise man and the fool alike.

Order: (1) 'Then I turned to consider wisdom and madness and folly' (v.12); (2) 'I saw that wisdom exceeds folly' (v.13); (3) 'one fate overcomes them both' (v.14); (4) 'So I hated life' (v.17); (5) 'Nothing is better for a man than to eat and drink and enjoy his work... this is from the hand of God' (v.24).
The phrase 'under the sun' recurs through the passage (vv.17-22). What horizon or realm is the Preacher pointing to with it?
'Under the sun' marks the horizon of this present life, where the whole trial of toil and wisdom is confined. Within that horizon he weighs everything and finds no lasting gain (vv.11, 17-22).
Verse 14 says 'one fate overcomes them both.' Using verse 16, what is the 'one fate' that befalls wise man and fool alike?
The one fate is death. Verse 16 makes it plain: 'the wise man will die just like the fool!' Both die, and both are forgotten in the days to come.

verse.en: For there is no lasting remembrance of the wise, just as with the fool, seeing that both will be forgotten in the days to come. Alas, the wise man will die just like the fool!

text.en: Verse 24 says the ability to eat, drink, and enjoy work is 'from the hand of God.' What does that phrase tell you about where such enjoyment comes from?

It tells us the enjoyment is a gift from God, not something a person can produce alone: 'this is from the hand of God' (v.24), and v.25 adds, 'For apart from Him, who can eat and who can find enjoyment?'
As the passage is read, listen for the short verdict the Preacher repeats after several of his thoughts.
The recurring verdict is 'This too is futile' (vv.19, 21, 23), often lengthened to 'futile and a pursuit of the wind' (v.26). The refrain carries his running assessment of toil under the sun.
Nothing is better for a man than to eat and drink and enjoy his work. I have also seen that this is from the hand of God. (Ecclesiastes 2:24, BSB)

And I saw that wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness: The wise man has eyes in his head, but the fool walks in darkness. Yet I also came to realize that one fate overcomes them both. (Ecclesiastes 2:13-14, BSB)
Finish the comparison from verse 13 from memory: 'And I saw that wisdom exceeds folly, just as ___.'
'...just as light exceeds darkness' (v.13).

verse.en: And I saw that wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness:

text.en: Retell the whole passage in your own words: how the Preacher weighed wisdom against folly, why he came to hate his toil, and where his thought finally landed.

A full retelling covers: the Preacher turning to consider wisdom and folly and granting that 'wisdom exceeds folly' (vv.12-13); the leveling realization that 'one fate overcomes them both' — both die and are forgotten (vv.14-16); his hatred of life and toil because he must leave all he built to a successor who may be wise or foolish (vv.17-21); the grief and sleepless nights such labor brings (vv.22-23); and the turn to the good that is 'from the hand of God' — to eat, drink, and enjoy one's work, given to the one pleasing in His sight (vv.24-26).
Verse 26 says the sinner gathers and accumulates 'that which he will hand over to one who pleases God.' How does Proverbs 13:22 state this same pattern?

Proverbs 13:22 (named in the brief) says, 'the sinner's wealth is passed to the righteous.' It states the same providential pattern as Ecclesiastes 2:26 — what the sinner heaps up is stored up for the one who pleases God. Scripture interprets Scripture.
Verse 24 says 'Nothing is better for a man than to eat and drink and enjoy his work.' How does Ecclesiastes 3:22 repeat this same conclusion later in the book?

Ecclesiastes 3:22 (named in the brief) says, 'there is nothing better for a man than to enjoy his work, because that is his lot.' It echoes the verdict of 2:24 — enjoying one's God-given work as one's portion. The refrain recurs across the book.

The Preacher finds that wisdom pursued 'under the sun' still ends in death and is forgotten (vv.14-16). How does 1 Corinthians 1:30-31 point beyond that horizon to where true wisdom and boasting are found?

1 Corinthians 1:30-31 (named in the brief) says Christ 'has become for us wisdom from God' and, 'Let him who boasts boast in the Lord.' Where the Preacher's wisdom under the sun cannot escape death, true wisdom is Christ, and the only lasting glory is in the Lord — beyond the horizon of vv.14-16.
When you hear the Preacher say his hard work felt 'futile,' what is one thing you would like to ask him about it?

Receive the child's own question; do not supply an answer. Good wonderings might touch why the work felt empty, what he was hoping to gain, or whether anything he did still mattered. Write the question down to carry into the reading.

The passage moves from despair over toil (vv.20-23) to 'Nothing is better... than to eat and drink and enjoy his work' (v.24). What question does that sudden turn raise for you that you would want to explore?

Invite the learner's own question; resist answering it for them. The aim is to surface genuine wondering about how the same labor can be both vanity and, received from God's hand, a gift to enjoy — not to test recall.

Hold verse 13, 'wisdom exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness,' against verse 14, 'one fate overcomes them both.' What real advantage does the Preacher grant to wisdom, and what limit does he then place on it?

First anchor to the text: he concedes a genuine advantage — wisdom truly 'exceeds folly, just as light exceeds darkness' (v.13); the wise man 'has eyes in his head' while 'the fool walks in darkness' (v.14). Then he limits it: 'one fate overcomes them both' (v.14) — death levels them, and 'the wise man will die just like the fool' (v.16). Wisdom is better to have, yet it cannot buy exemption from death. Keep the point to the text's own concession-then-limit; do not resolve it beyond what the passage states.

After all the talk of vanity, verse 24 says of eating, drinking, and enjoying work, 'this is from the hand of God.' How does naming God shift the passage from despair to something the Preacher can call good?

Anchor first to what the text says: the enjoyment of ordinary work and food is 'from the hand of God' (v.24), and 'apart from Him' none can find it (v.25). Naming God is the hinge: the same toil that was 'futile' under the sun becomes a gift when received from His hand. The turn is not to a new labor but to a new source — God's providence and grace. Meaning drawn from the text's own hinge, not speculation.
Verses 18-21 tell why the Preacher hated his toil. According to the text, what was it about leaving his work behind that troubled him so deeply?

Anchor to the text: he hated his toil because 'I must leave it to the man who comes after me' (v.18), and 'who knows whether that man will be wise or foolish?' (v.19). One who 'has not worked for it' receives the portion he built 'with wisdom, knowledge, and skill' (v.21) — 'this too is futile and a great evil.' What troubles him is the loss of control over the fruit of his skilled labor once he is gone. Keep the reading to what the passage states.

On an index card, write one part of your daily work or study that you will deliberately receive this week as a gift 'from the hand of God' (v.24), and beside it write one specific way you will give thanks for it. Keep the card where you will see it.

Doable on paper with an index card and pencil. Ties v.24's 'from the hand of God' into a concrete, thankful use of one's calling. Note the planned action and the thanksgiving as observable evidence; never record a judgment about the learner's gratitude or spiritual state.

Verse 23 describes labor so anxious that 'even at night, his mind does not rest.' Name one specific task you tend to carry into the night, and one concrete thing you will do this week to set it down and rest.

Look for a concrete, doable step tied to a real task named by the learner (v.23). Record only the observable plan; do not assess the learner's anxiety, faith, or character. The aim is the ordered, cheerful use of one's calling, not a verdict on the heart.

Draw a picture of one good thing you enjoy that is a gift from God — food you like to eat, or a job you help with at home. Point to your picture and tell what you drew and how you can say thank you to God for it.

Ties verse 24's good gifts 'from the hand of God' into a doable drawing with paper and crayons. Affirm the concrete thing the child names and their idea of giving thanks; record what they drew as observable evidence, never a judgment about the child.
In this passage, who appoints a time for every purpose, lays the burden upon men, and makes everything beautiful in its time?
God. He appoints "a time for every purpose under heaven" (3:1), "has laid upon the sons of men" their burden (3:10), and "has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11).
The Preacher says a burden has been laid on people to occupy them, and that God has set eternity in their hearts. On whom does the passage say these things fall?

On "the sons of men" / "men": God "has laid upon the sons of men" the burden "to occupy them" (3:10), and "has also set eternity in the hearts of men" (3:11). The one who toils is also called "the worker" (3:9).
After the long list of paired 'times' (3:2-8), what question does the Preacher ask next, and what does he turn to consider after asking it?

He first asks, "What does the worker gain from his toil?" (3:9). He then turns from the catalogue of times to reflect on God's work: the burden God has laid on men (3:10), that God "has made everything beautiful in its time" and set eternity in the heart (3:11), the good of rejoicing and finding satisfaction as God's gift (3:12-13), and that God's work endures forever so that men should fear Him (3:14-15).
The passage repeats the words 'season' and 'time' (3:1). In the sense this passage gives them, does a 'time' mean a moment we happen to choose, or something else? Explain from the text.

A 'time' or 'season' here means a divinely fixed, appointed span — not human choice or chance. The passage grounds every 'time' in God: there is "a time for every purpose under heaven" (3:1), and God "has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11). The paired opposites (3:2-8) are seasons that each take their turn under God's appointment, which is why toil adds no lasting gain (3:9) and God's work "endures forever" (3:14).
Verse 11 says God "has made everything beautiful in its time." What do the words "in its time" add to the meaning here?

"In its time" means each thing is fitting and lovely in its own proper season — as cold suits winter and heat suits summer. God's beauty is not that every event is pleasant, but that each is set in the right season by His ordering, so it becomes "beautiful in its time." This matches the appointed 'seasons' of 3:1-8.
Listen for the two small words that begin almost every line in the first part of the reading. What word keeps coming back before 'to be born,' 'to plant,' 'to laugh'?

"A time" — the phrase "a time to..." begins each half of the paired opposites in 3:2-8 (e.g., "a time to be born and a time to die," "a time to weep and a time to laugh"). "Time" (with "season") is the passage's key repeated term (3:1).
To everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under heaven: (Ecclesiastes 3:1)
He has made everything beautiful in its time. He has also set eternity in the hearts of men, yet they cannot fathom the work that God has done from beginning to end. (Ecclesiastes 3:11)
Finish the line from verse 11: 'He has made everything ____ in its time.'
"beautiful" — "He has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11).

This passage ends by saying God "will call to account what has passed" (3:15). Two verses later, Ecclesiastes 3:17 picks up the same thought. What does Ecclesiastes 3:17 say God will do, and how does it use the idea of appointed 'time'?

Ecclesiastes 3:17 says, in the Preacher's heart, that "God will judge the righteous and the wicked, since there is a time for every activity and every deed." It applies the same appointed-'time' theme of 3:1-8 to judgment: just as every purpose has its season, so God's judging has its set time. This confirms 3:15 that God "will call to account what has passed."

Verse 11 says God "has made everything beautiful in its time," showing His ordered wisdom. Romans 1:19-20 points to another place people can see God's wisdom and power. Where does Romans say it is seen?

Romans 1:19-20 says that God's invisible qualities — His eternal power and divine nature — have been clearly seen since the creation of the world, being understood from what He has made, so that men are without excuse. As Ecclesiastes sees God's ordered wisdom in making everything "beautiful in its time" (3:11), Romans sees it in the created world — Scripture interpreting Scripture.

The Preacher says God "has also set eternity in the hearts of men, yet they cannot fathom the work that God has done from beginning to end" (3:11). What questions does this raise for you about the things you long to understand but cannot?

Open item — invite the learner's own wondering; do not supply a settled answer. Good directions to welcome: why God gives us a sense of eternity yet not full understanding; what it means to trust God with what we cannot fathom (3:11); how this drives us to "fear Him" (3:14) rather than to despair. Keep the response the learner's own; record what they ask, not a verdict on their faith.
The poem names many opposite times — "a time to weep and a time to laugh, a time to mourn and a time to dance." What is one thing you would want to ask about how God gives us these different times?

Open item — the child's own question is the goal; do not answer it for them. It touches the appointed 'seasons' of 3:1-8 and God making everything "beautiful in its time" (3:11). Welcome wonder about weeping and laughing seasons alike; write down the question the child asks. Evidence, not judgment.

The passage lists opposite pairs such as "a time to kill and a time to heal" and "a time for war and a time for peace." Read alongside verse 1 and verse 11, what one truth does the whole list teach — and why would it be a mistake to read it as approving both sides of every pair equally?

Anchor first in the text: "To everything there is a season, and a time for every purpose under heaven" (3:1) and "He has made everything beautiful in its time" (3:11). The one truth: every event and condition of life has a season fixed by God's sovereign appointment, and He orders each in its proper time. The pairs illustrate that single truth — the ceaseless turns of a life we cannot control. They are not a moral endorsement of both terms (the passage is not commending killing or hating); it observes that each has its appointed season under God, driving us to "fear Him" (3:14). Reading move: read each pair as an instance of the one sovereign appointment, not as ethics. Keep to what the text states (WCF 1.9).
Verse 14 says "everything God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it or taken from it." According to the same verse, why does God do it this way — what does the text say His purpose is?

The text states it plainly: "God does it so that they should fear Him" (3:14). Anchor: God's work "endures forever" and cannot be added to or diminished (3:14); its unchangeable permanence is meant to lead men to fear (reverence and awe) Him. This is the end God intends by the mystery of His providence — meaning drawn from the verse itself, not speculation.
Retell this passage in your own words: the many appointed 'times' of life, the question about what the worker gains, and what the Preacher then says about God's work and the good gift God gives.

A faithful retelling should include: every purpose has its God-appointed season (3:1), shown by the paired opposites (3:2-8); the worker gains no lasting profit from his toil (3:9), a burden God has laid on men (3:10); yet God makes everything beautiful in its time and has set eternity in the heart, though man cannot fathom His whole work (3:11); so the good is to rejoice, do good, eat, drink, and find satisfaction in labor as God's gift (3:12-13); and God's work endures forever, unchangeable, so that men should fear Him (3:14-15). Do not require exact wording; listen for these movements. Record what the learner recalls, not a judgment of them.

Verses 12-13 say there is nothing better than "to rejoice and do good" and to "find satisfaction" in our work as God's gift. What is one specific good thing you could do this week, and one task you could do thankfully instead of grumbling?

Application — look for a concrete, doable action, not a feeling or self-assessment. Anchor: "there is nothing better for them than to rejoice and do good while they live" and to "eat and drink and find satisfaction in all his labor—this is the gift of God" (3:12-13). Good answers name a specific deed (help a sibling, thank a parent) and a specific task done with gladness. Record the observable plan; do not grade the child's heart.

On an index card, write one 'time' from the poem that fits something happening in your family right now (for example, "a time to mend" or "a time to plant"). On the back, write one good, doable thing you can do in that season this week.

Paper activity — an index card and pencil are enough. It moves the appointed 'times' of 3:1-8 into the learner's present life and pairs it with the 3:12-13 good of doing good and working faithfully as God's gift. Look for a concrete action tied to a real family season. Keep the record to what was written and planned; observe action, never assess character.
Draw a picture of one 'time' from the reading — a time to plant, a time to laugh, or a time to dance. Then act it out for your family.

Paper-and-crayon activity for pre-readers; the drawing and acting-out are the observable response. It fixes one of the appointed 'times' (3:2-4) in memory and joins it to the passage's call to "rejoice and do good" (3:12). No reading required — an adult can name the times aloud. Record which 'time' the child chose and drew; do not assess the child.
When Solomon looked under the sun, in what two places did he see wickedness?
In "the place of judgment" and in "the place of righteousness" (3:16) — the very courts that should have held righteousness held wickedness instead.
The passage names who will do the judging and who is being tested. Who is each?
God is the judge — "God will judge the righteous and the wicked" (3:17). The "sons of men" are the ones God tests, "so that they may see for themselves that they are but beasts" (3:18).
As the passage is read, listen for the one place the passage says everyone goes, and the one thing everyone comes from and returns to.
"All go to one place; all come from dust, and all return to dust" (3:20).
Verse 20 traces where all people and animals come from and where they end up. In order, what does it say — where do all come from, and where do all return?
The order is: all "come from dust," and all "return to dust"; and all "go to one place" (3:20). Origin in dust, then return to dust.
Solomon says he "saw under the sun" what was happening. In this passage, what vantage point does the phrase "under the sun" describe?

The phrase "under the sun" names the view of life on earth taken by itself — what can be seen in this world apart from looking above it. From that vantage alone, wickedness sits in the courts and death seems to level man and beast (3:16, 3:19).
In verse 18 Solomon says "God tests them so that they may see for themselves that they are but beasts." What does this testing show the sons of men about themselves?

Anchor first to the text: v18 says the testing is "so that they may see for themselves that they are but beasts," and v19 explains the likeness — the same breath and the same death. The interpretation to draw out: the testing brings men to see their own frailty and mortality, that in their bodily life, subject to death, they are like the beasts (3:18-19). Per the brief, the beast-comparison holds as regards liability to death only, not the soul; note what the learner reasons from the text before adding this.
Verse 19 says men and animals "all have the same breath." What is the one thing this verse says men and animals share?
The "same breath" — the same bodily life, and so the same death: "As one dies, so dies the other" (3:19).
Verse 22 says that to enjoy his work is a man's "lot." What does the word "lot" mean here?

A person's portion or allotted share in this life — the good God gives him to enjoy in his work: "there is nothing better for a man than to enjoy his work, because that is his lot" (3:22). The brief frames it as thankful, cheerful enjoyment of God's gifts, founded on the fear of God.
As the passage is read, listen for the one word repeated in verse 20 for what all creatures are made from and return to.
The word is "dust" — "all come from dust, and all return to dust" (3:20).
All go to one place; all come from dust, and all return to dust. (Ecclesiastes 3:20)
I said in my heart, “God will judge the righteous and the wicked, since there is a time for every activity and every deed.” (Ecclesiastes 3:17)
Fill in the words from verse 20 — All come from ____, and all return to ____.
"dust" both times: "all come from dust, and all return to dust" (3:20).

In verse 21 the skeptic shrugs, "Who knows if the spirit of man rises upward and the spirit of the animal descends into the earth?" Later in Ecclesiastes, which verse answers this by telling where the spirit of man goes?

Ecclesiastes 12:7 answers it: "the dust returns to the ground from which it came and the spirit returns to God who gave it." Scripture interprets Scripture (WCF 1.9): the unanswered "Who knows" of 3:21 is settled within the same book — man's spirit returns to God.
Verse 20 says all "return to dust." Which earlier verse in Genesis first spoke these words to Adam?
Genesis 3:19, where God tells Adam that for dust he is, and to dust he shall return. Solomon's "return to dust" (3:20) echoes God's word to Adam at the fall.
Verse 17 says "God will judge the righteous and the wicked" (3:17). Name one other verse the brief points to that also declares this coming judgment.

Ecclesiastes 12:14 — "For God will bring every deed into judgment, along with every hidden thing, whether good or evil." (2 Corinthians 5:10 likewise declares that we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ.) Either confirms the coming judgment 3:17 names.
Verse 21 asks, "Who knows if the spirit of man rises upward and the spirit of the animal descends into the earth?" What is one question this verse makes you want to ask? Write it down.

Invite the learner's own wondering; do not supply an answer here. Good questions might touch what happens after death, how man differs from the animals, or why Solomon phrases it as a question. If a learner wants the answer, note that 3:17 and 12:7 speak to it — but let the question stand first.
The passage talks about people and animals, dust, and God as judge. What is one thing here you would like to ask God about? Draw or say your question.

Welcome any genuine question the child raises; the aim is that they practice asking, not that they land on a set answer. Record the question they ask as evidence of their wondering; do not evaluate it as right or wrong.

In verses 19-21 Solomon says man "has no advantage over the animals" and asks "Who knows" where the spirit goes. Does the passage itself leave man level with the beasts? Point to the verse that keeps them apart.

The beast-comparison holds only as regards liability to death — the body shares dust and breath (3:19-20). But the passage does not stop there: 3:17 already declares "God will judge the righteous and the wicked," which no beast faces, and the "Who knows" of 3:21 is a lament over how few consider the difference, not a denial of it (brief, JFB). Read with 12:7, the spirit of man returns to God. Anchor learners to what the text states (3:17) before asking why.
After looking at injustice and death, verse 22 says "there is nothing better for a man than to enjoy his work" (3:22). Why does Solomon land on enjoying one's work as the conclusion?

Anchor first to the text: 3:22 grounds it in "because that is his lot" and "who can bring him to see what will come after him?" Since the future is hidden and toil is his allotted portion, thankful enjoyment of God's gifts in the present is the fitting response (brief: founded on the fear of God, not sensualism), rather than despair over what "under the sun" cannot answer.
Retell this passage (Ecclesiastes 3:16-22) in your own words: what Solomon saw wrong, what he says God will do, how he compares men and beasts, and what he concludes is best.

Listen for the movement of thought: injustice in the courts (v16) answered by coming judgment (v17); the testing that shows men their mortal frailty like beasts (v18-21, true as to death); and the conclusion that nothing is better than to enjoy one's work as one's lot (v22). Note which parts the learner recovers; gaps show where to review, not a verdict on the learner.

Verse 22 says it is good to enjoy your work. On paper, draw or list one kind of work you do this week (a chore, schoolwork, helping at home), and next to it write one thing you can thank God for in that work.

Observable output: a drawing or short list plus a thanksgiving. Record what the child produced (e.g., 'listed sweeping and named being able to help'), not a judgment about gratitude. Pre-reader variant: let them draw the work and say the thank-you aloud for a helper to write.
Draw a picture of one job you get to do (feeding a pet, picking up toys, helping cook). Then say one word for how it feels to help. A grown-up can write your word by the picture.
Observable output: a drawing and a spoken word captured by the helper. This connects to 3:22's 'enjoy his work.' Record what was drawn and said; do not assess the child's attitude or heart.

Verse 17 says "God will judge the righteous and the wicked, since there is a time for every activity and every deed." Name one ordinary action this week you could do differently in light of God's coming judgment. What is the concrete step?

Aim for one concrete, observable step (e.g., 'correct an error I let a customer overpay for,' 'finish a task I was cutting corners on'). Record the action the person names; the point is a doable response to the coming judgment 3:17 names, not an evaluation of their spiritual state. (Ecclesiastes 12:14 states the scope directly: God brings every deed into judgment.)
In verse 1 the writer looks at people who are being treated cruelly. What does the passage say twice about whether these oppressed people had anyone to comfort them, and in whose hands was the power?
They had no one to comfort them: the passage says twice "they had no comforter" and "there was no comforter" (4:1). And "the power lay in the hands of their oppressors" (4:1).
Verses 7-8 describe one particular man the Preacher sees. What does the passage say about his family, and about how he feels toward his wealth?

He is "a man all alone, without even a son or brother" (4:8). Though "there is no end to his labor," his "eyes are still not content with his wealth," and he asks, "For whom do I toil and bereave my soul of enjoyment?" (4:8).
In verses 13-16 the Preacher contrasts two people. Who are they, and what does the passage say about each one's wisdom and station?

A "poor but wise youth" is set against "an old but foolish king who no longer knows how to take a warning" (4:13). The youth "has come from the prison to the kingship, though he was born poor in his own kingdom" (4:14), and became "the youth who succeeded the king" (4:15).

Verses 13-16 trace the rise and end of the wise youth. Put these stages in the order they happened in his life: crowds follow him, born poor, comes to the kingship, comes from prison, is not celebrated by those who come later.

The sequence of his life (the order events happened, not the clause order of v.14, which names prison and kingship before the parenthetical "born poor"): (1) "born poor in his own kingdom" (4:14); (2) "come from the prison" (4:14); (3) "to the kingship," succeeding the king (4:14-15); (4) "all who lived and walked under the sun followed this second one" (4:15); (5) "the successor will not be celebrated by those who come even later" (4:16). Even this rise ends in "futile and a pursuit of the wind" (4:16).
The passage repeats the phrase "under the sun" (4:1, 4:3, 4:7, 4:15). In the sense this passage gives it, what realm or vantage point does "under the sun" name?

"Under the sun" names the whole scene of human life on this earth as the Preacher observes it: the sphere of toil, oppression, wealth, and ambition where everything he sees runs out into what is "futile" and "a pursuit of the wind." It is the earthly, this-world vantage from which he looks and sees (4:1, 4:7) the evil "that is done under the sun" (4:3) and the crowds who "lived and walked under the sun" (4:15). Meaning drawn from the passage's own repeated use, not imported speculation.
Verse 1 says the oppressed "had no comforter." In the sense the passage gives it, what does it mean that they had "no comforter"?

To have "no comforter" means the oppressed were left utterly alone in their grief: no one to relieve them, take their side, or console their tears. The passage underscores their aloneness by saying it twice, "they had no comforter" and "there was no comforter" (4:1), while "the power lay in the hands of their oppressors." It describes their friendless, unhelped condition, not their inner character.
Verse 12 ends, "a cord of three strands is not quickly broken." Within verses 9-12, what point is this picture making, and why "three" rather than one?

Anchor in the text's own image: the picture teaches the strength of joined lives over the weakness of the solitary. A single thread snaps easily; strands twisted together hold. It caps the reasons of 4:9-12: "two are better than one" for their labor (4:9), for lifting one who falls (4:10), for warmth (4:11), and for resisting an attacker (4:12). "Three" heightens the point beyond even two, picturing companionship bound so firmly it "is not quickly broken." Keep to the text's own image of union against isolation, not allegory it does not state. Record what the learner sees in the picture, not a judgment of them.
Listen for the phrase the Preacher says again and again after he looks at something that does not last. He calls it a pursuit of the ____. What word fills the blank?
"the wind" - the Preacher repeats "a pursuit of the wind" (4:4, 4:6, 4:16), each time paired with calling the thing "futile." It is the passage's key repeated verdict on things that do not last.
Two are better than one, because they have a good return for their labor. (Ecclesiastes 4:9)
And though one may be overpowered, two can resist. Moreover, a cord of three strands is not quickly broken. (Ecclesiastes 4:12)
Finish the line from verse 6: 'Better one handful with ____ than two handfuls with toil and pursuit of the wind.'
"tranquility" - "Better one handful with tranquility than two handfuls with toil and pursuit of the wind" (4:6).
Verses 9-12 praise companionship over being alone. Genesis 2:18 gives the reason God said this from the beginning at creation. What does Genesis 2:18 say?

Genesis 2:18 says, "The LORD God also said, 'It is not good for the man to be alone. I will make for him a suitable helper.'" This grounds Ecclesiastes 4:9-12: the goodness of two over one traces back to God's own word at creation that solitude is "not good" - Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9).
Verse 12 says two joined together are strong. Proverbs 27:17 names another good that comes from companions. What does Proverbs 27:17 say?

Proverbs 27:17 says, "As iron sharpens iron, so one man sharpens another." It adds to Ecclesiastes 4:9-12 another benefit of companionship: friends do not only lift and strengthen one another (4:10, 4:12), they also sharpen one another. Both passages commend joined lives over the solitary.
Verse 6 prefers "one handful with tranquility" to "two handfuls with toil." Psalm 37:16 makes a similar comparison about a small amount versus a large one. What does Psalm 37:16 say is better?

Psalm 37:16 says, "Better is the little of the righteous than the abundance of many who are wicked." Like Ecclesiastes 4:6, which prefers "one handful with tranquility" to "two handfuls with toil and pursuit of the wind," the psalm counts a quiet little better than a troubled abundance - the same weighing of contentment over anxious gain.
The passage says the hurting people "had no comforter" (4:1). What is one thing you would want to ask about people who are sad or mistreated and have no one to comfort them?

Open item - the child's own question is the goal; do not answer it for them. It arises from the oppressed who "had no comforter" (4:1). Welcome wonderings such as why people are left without comfort, who could comfort them, or what God thinks of it. Write down the question the child asks; record wonder, never a judgment of the child.
The solitary man asks, "For whom do I toil and bereave my soul of enjoyment?" (4:8). What questions does his own question stir up in you about work and what it is for?

Open item - invite the learner's own wondering; do not supply a settled answer. It is prompted by the miser's cry, "For whom do I toil and bereave my soul of enjoyment?" (4:8). Good directions to welcome: who our labor is really for, why endless gaining can leave the soul empty, what it means that his "eyes are still not content with his wealth." Keep the response the learner's own; record what they ask, not a verdict on their heart.
Verses 9-12 give several reasons "two are better than one." List the reasons the passage states, then say what one truth about companionship they are meant to teach.

Anchor first in what the text states. The reasons: "they have a good return for their labor" (4:9); if one falls, "his companion can lift him up" (4:10); "if two lie down together, they will keep warm" (4:11); "though one may be overpowered, two can resist," and "a cord of three strands is not quickly broken" (4:12). The one truth: God made companionship a real good and strength, so that the solitary life the Preacher has just lamented (4:7-8) is poorer and weaker than a shared one. This is the passage's positive center against the surrounding vanities. Keep to what the text states (WCF 1.9); do not moralize beyond it.
Verse 6 sits between the worker driven by envy (v.4) and the idle fool who folds his hands (v.5). What middle way does verse 6 commend, and why does it call that way "better"?

Anchor in the text: "Better one handful with tranquility than two handfuls with toil and pursuit of the wind" (4:6). The middle way is quiet, contented labor - a "handful with tranquility" - set between two errors the passage has just named: the envious striving that makes "all labor and success spring from a man's envy of his neighbor" (4:4), and the idle fool who "folds his hands and consumes his own flesh" (4:5). It is "better" because the peace of the smaller portion outweighs the anxious "toil and pursuit of the wind" of grasping for more. Meaning drawn from the verse in its setting, not speculation.

Retell this passage in your own words: the oppressed with no comforter, the striving worker and the idle fool with the better handful between them, the lonely man who toils for no one, the strength of two together, and the wise youth whose rise is soon forgotten.

A faithful retelling should include: the oppressed who "had no comforter" while power sits with oppressors (4:1-3); labor springing from "envy of his neighbor" (4:4), the idle fool (4:5), and the "better" handful with tranquility (4:6); the solitary man who toils endlessly yet asks "for whom do I toil" (4:7-8); the good of companionship - "two are better than one," lifting, warming, resisting, "a cord of three strands" (4:9-12); and the poor wise youth who rises from prison to kingship yet is soon forgotten (4:13-16), the whole strung on the refrain "futile" and "a pursuit of the wind." Do not require exact wording; listen for these movements. Record what the learner recalls, not a judgment of them.
Verse 10 says a companion "can lift him up" when he falls. Name one specific person you could help this week, and one concrete thing you could do to lift them up.

Application - look for a concrete, doable action, not a feeling or self-assessment. Anchor: "if one falls down, his companion can lift him up; but pity the one who falls without another to help him up" (4:10). Good answers name a specific person and a specific deed (help a younger sibling who is stuck, sit with a lonely classmate, carry something for a parent). Record the observable plan; do not grade the child's heart.

On an index card, draw a cord of three strands like verse 12. On each strand write the name of one person who helps you stand (family, friend, church). On the back, write one concrete way you will help lift up one of them this week.

Paper activity - an index card and pencil are enough. It moves "a cord of three strands is not quickly broken" and "two are better than one" (4:9-12) into the learner's real relationships and pairs it with a concrete act of helping ("his companion can lift him up," 4:10). Look for a specific, doable action tied to a named person. Keep the record to what was written and planned; observe action, never assess character.
Draw a picture of two people, where one has fallen down and the other is helping them stand up again, like verse 10. Then show your family and tell who you could help.

Paper-and-crayon activity for pre-readers; the drawing and telling are the observable response. It fixes "if one falls down, his companion can lift him up" (4:10) in memory. No reading required - an adult can say the line aloud. Record whom the child says they could help; do not assess the child.
The Preacher tells the reader to guard his steps when he goes to one particular place. What place does verse 1 name?
"the house of God" — "Guard your steps when you go to the house of God" (5:1). It is the place of worship, where one is to "Draw near to listen" (5:1).
When the passage tells us to let our words be few, it names where God is and where we are. According to verse 2, where is each?
"God is in heaven and you are on earth" (5:2). This contrast is the passage's reason for reverent, few words: "After all, God is in heaven and you are on earth. So let your words be few" (5:2).
Listen for the place the Preacher says to guard your steps when you go to it. What place is it?
"the house of God" (5:1) — the place of worship. The child should also hear that we go there to "Draw near to listen" (5:1).
Verse 1 says, "Guard your steps when you go to the house of God." In this passage, what does "guard your steps" call for as we come to worship?

It calls for careful, reverent, composed approach to worship — watching how one comes, with thoughts and affections gathered, rather than careless or hasty. The passage itself defines it by contrast: "Draw near to listen rather than to offer the sacrifice of fools, who do not know that they do wrong" (5:1). So to "guard your steps" is to come attentively to hear and obey, not to rest in mere outward performance.
The passage commands, "So let your words be few" (5:2). What kind of speech before God is it commending, and what is it warning against?

It commends reverent, deliberate, thoughtful speech before God — not many hasty or careless words. It warns against the fool's babbling: "Do not be quick to speak, and do not be hasty in your heart to utter a word before God" (5:2), and "As a dream comes through many cares, so the speech of a fool comes with many words" (5:3), summed up again in "so do many words. Therefore, fear God" (5:7). "Few" here means measured and reverent, weighed before the God who "is in heaven" (5:2) — not mere brevity for its own sake.
The passage ends its instruction, "Therefore, fear God" (5:7). Judging from the whole passage, what does it mean to "fear God" here?

To "fear God" here is to hold Him in reverent awe as the God who "is in heaven" while "you are on earth" (5:2), and so to worship, speak, and vow accordingly. It is the root command the whole passage drives toward: guard your steps and draw near to listen (5:1), let your words be few (5:2), and pay your vows in full (5:4-6) — all "because" one fears God (5:7). It is not fright but reverence that governs both empty worship and hasty words.
Do not be quick to speak, and do not be hasty in your heart to utter a word before God. After all, God is in heaven and you are on earth. So let your words be few. (Ecclesiastes 5:2)
For as many dreams bring futility, so do many words. Therefore, fear God. (Ecclesiastes 5:7)
It is better not to vow than to make a vow and not fulfill it. (Ecclesiastes 5:5)
Fill in the blank from verse 1: Draw near to ____ rather than to offer the sacrifice of fools.
"listen" — "Draw near to listen rather than to offer the sacrifice of fools, who do not know that they do wrong" (5:1).
Verse 2 warns against hasty, many words before God and says, "let your words be few." Jesus gives a like warning about prayer in Matthew 6:7. What does Matthew 6:7 tell us not to do, and why?

Matthew 6:7 warns against praying by babbling on like pagans, who think that by their many words they will be heard. It matches Ecclesiastes 5:2-3: God is not moved by a flood of words, and "the speech of a fool comes with many words" (5:3). Both texts teach reverent, deliberate prayer over mere quantity of words — Scripture interpreting Scripture (WCF 1.9).
Verse 1 says, "Draw near to listen," and verse 2, "Do not be quick to speak." James 1:19 gives a similar order for how we should be. What three things does James 1:19 say we should be?

James 1:19 says everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to anger. This echoes Ecclesiastes 5:1-2: "Draw near to listen" and "Do not be quick to speak." Both put hearing before speaking as the mark of reverence and wisdom — Scripture agreeing with Scripture.
Verses 4-5 command that a vow made to God be fulfilled and not delayed. Numbers 30:2 states the same law about vows. What does Numbers 30:2 require of the one who makes a vow?

Numbers 30:2 says that if a man makes a vow to the LORD or swears an oath to obligate himself by a pledge, he must not break his word; he must do everything he has promised. This is the law standing behind Ecclesiastes 5:4-5: "When you make a vow to God, do not delay in fulfilling it"... "Fulfill your vow" and "It is better not to vow than to make a vow and not fulfill it." A vow is a binding obligation to be paid in full — the two texts teach one doctrine.
The passage says, "God is in heaven and you are on earth. So let your words be few" (5:2). What questions does this raise for you about how you speak to God when you pray?

Open item — the learner's own question is the goal; do not supply a settled answer. It touches 5:2-3, where reverence for the God who "is in heaven" leads to few, weighed words rather than a fool's many words. Welcome wonderings about what reverent prayer looks like, why fewer careful words can matter more than many, or how to listen before speaking (5:1). Record the question the learner asks, not a judgment of their prayers.
The Preacher says, "It is better not to vow than to make a vow and not fulfill it" (5:5). What do you find yourself wanting to ask about making and keeping promises to God?

Open item — invite the learner's own wondering; do not answer it for them. It arises from 5:4-6: pay a vow promptly and in full, and do not excuse a broken one before "the messenger" (5:6). Good directions to welcome: what counts as a vow to God, why an unfulfilled vow is treated so seriously, or how the fear of God (5:7) frees us from rash promises. Write down the question the learner asks; observe, do not assess their faith.

The passage gives many commands — guard your steps, draw near to listen, let your words be few, fulfill your vow. It then closes, "Therefore, fear God" (5:7). What does the text show is the single root these many commands grow from, and how do the worship section (vv1-3) and the vow section (vv4-6) both trace back to it?

Anchor first in the text: "Therefore, fear God" (5:7) is the passage's own conclusion, marked by "Therefore." The single root is the fear of God — reverent awe of the One who "is in heaven" while "you are on earth" (5:2). From it grow both halves: the worship commands (guard your steps, draw near to listen, few words, vv1-3) and the vow commands (do not delay, fulfill your vow, do not call it a mistake, vv4-6). Reading move: read every imperative as an outworking of fearing God, not as separate rules; the proverb-figures at v3 and v7 (dreams and many words) press the same point about the fool's careless speech. Keep to what the text states (WCF 1.9); this fear is reverence, the remedy for both empty worship and rash words.

Verse 3 says, "As a dream comes through many cares, so the speech of a fool comes with many words," and verse 7 says, "as many dreams bring futility, so do many words." What point about a fool's speech is this comparison to dreams making?

Anchor in the text: the image sets dreams beside a fool's many words (5:3, 5:7). The point: just as a dream is the empty, restless overflow of a mind full of "many cares," so a fool's flood of "many words" is empty and comes to "futility." Both are unsubstantial noise. The comparison serves the argument of the whole passage — that reverent, few words fit the God who "is in heaven" (5:2), so "fear God" (5:7). Reading move: treat the dream-image as illustration serving the argument, not a standalone metaphor to mine. Stay with what the text states (WCF 1.9).

Retell this passage in your own words: how the Preacher says to come to the house of God, what he says about our words before God, what he says about making and keeping vows, and the one command he ends with.

A faithful retelling should include: guard your steps and draw near to listen rather than offer the sacrifice of fools (5:1); do not be quick or hasty to speak, for God is in heaven and you are on earth, so let your words be few (5:2), since a fool's speech comes with many words (5:3); when you vow to God, do not delay but fulfill it — better not to vow than to vow and not fulfill it (5:4-5) — and do not let your mouth cause you to sin or call your vow a mistake before the messenger (5:6); and the closing command, "fear God" (5:7). The oppression note (5:8) may or may not appear. Do not require exact wording; listen for these movements. Record what the learner recalls, not a judgment of them.

Verse 1 says to "Draw near to listen" and verse 2 says, "Do not be quick to speak." This week when your family reads the Bible or prays together, what is one specific thing you could do to listen carefully instead of speaking too quickly?

Application — look for a concrete, doable action, not a feeling or self-assessment. Anchor: "Draw near to listen" (5:1) and "Do not be quick to speak" (5:2). Good answers name a specific, observable step (sit still and quiet during the reading, wait until the reader finishes before asking a question, write down one thing heard). Record the observable plan; do not grade the child's heart or attention.

On an index card, write one promise you have made (to God, a parent, or a friend) that you have not yet kept. On the back, write the specific day and step by which you will "Fulfill your vow" (5:4) this week.

Paper activity — an index card and pencil are enough. It moves 5:4-5 ("do not delay in fulfilling it"... "Fulfill your vow"; "better not to vow than to make a vow and not fulfill it") into a concrete, doable step. Look for a specific promise and a specific day/action to keep it, not a general resolve. Keep the record to what was written and planned; observe action, never assess character or spiritual state.
Draw a picture of yourself sitting quietly with your Bible, listening. Then practice folding your hands and being still and quiet, the way we "draw near to listen" when God's Word is read.

Paper-and-crayon activity for pre-readers; the drawing and the practice of sitting still are the observable response. It fixes 5:1 ("Draw near to listen") in a doable form. No reading required — an adult names the action aloud. Record that the child drew the picture and practiced listening quietly; do not assess the child's heart or reverence.
In verse 9 the passage says the produce of the earth is taken by all, but it names one person in particular who also profits from the fields. Who is it?
The king. Verse 9 says 'the king himself profits from the fields' — even the ruler depends on the common produce of the land.
Verse 12 contrasts two people by how they sleep. Whose sleep does the passage call sweet, and whose wealth lets him get no sleep?
The worker's — 'The sleep of the worker is sweet' — while 'the abundance of the rich man permits him no sleep' (v.12).
According to verse 15, in what condition does a man arrive in the world, in what condition does he depart, and what does he carry away with him?
He comes 'naked' from his mother’s womb and departs 'naked as he arrived'; the passage adds, 'He takes nothing for his labor to carry in his hands' — nothing goes with him (v.15).
Verse 10 says the person who loves money is 'never satisfied.' What does 'never satisfied' tell us about someone who loves money?

That no amount is ever enough: 'He who loves money is never satisfied by money.' The craving for more has no resting point, unlike a natural hunger a meal can fill; the passage calls this 'futile' (v.10).
In verse 16 the Preacher asks what a man gains as he 'toils for the wind.' What does the picture of toiling 'for the wind' say about such labor?

That it grasps nothing solid — wind cannot be caught or kept. 'Toils for the wind' pictures effort spent for what is insubstantial and cannot be carried away, matching v.15's 'He takes nothing for his labor to carry in his hands.'
Verse 19 ends, 'This is a gift from God.' Reading verses 18-19 together, what exactly does the passage call God's gift?

Not merely the riches themselves, but the power to enjoy them. God 'has given riches and wealth to every man, and He has enabled him to enjoy them, to accept his lot, and to rejoice in his labor.' The gift is the God-given ability to eat, drink, and find satisfaction in one's lot (vv.18-19).
He who loves money is never satisfied by money, and he who loves wealth is never satisfied by income. This too is futile. (Ecclesiastes 5:10)
Furthermore, God has given riches and wealth to every man, and He has enabled him to enjoy them, to accept his lot, and to rejoice in his labor. This is a gift from God. (Ecclesiastes 5:19)
Fill in the missing word from verse 15: 'As a man came from his mother’s womb, so he will ______ again, naked as he arrived.'
depart. The full line reads, 'As a man came from his mother’s womb, so he will depart again, naked as he arrived' (v.15).
Verse 10 warns that the one who loves money is never satisfied. The brief points to 1 Timothy 6:10. How does 1 Timothy 6:10 describe the love of money?

1 Timothy 6:10 says the love of money is the root of all kinds of evil, and that by craving it some have wandered from the faith and pierced themselves with many sorrows — the same restless craving the Preacher exposes here as never satisfied (v.10).
Verse 15 says a man departs 'naked as he arrived' and 'takes nothing' with him. The brief connects this to Job 1:21. What does Job say there about coming and going with nothing?

Job 1:21 says, 'Naked I came from my mother’s womb, and naked I will return. The LORD gave, and the LORD has taken away.' Job voices the same truth the Preacher observes — we arrive and depart empty-handed — and, like Ecclesiastes 5:19, traces both having and losing to God's hand (v.15).
Verse 12 says the rich man's wealth keeps him from sleeping. What is one question you would like to ask about why having more can bring less rest?

Let the learner voice their own wondering; do not supply the answer. The passage states the fact plainly (v.12) — the rich man's abundance 'permits him no sleep' — without over-explaining. Fruitful wonderings may touch worry, the burden of keeping wealth, or never having enough (cf. v.10). Record the question the learner actually asks; do not judge the child's contentment.
Verse 13 calls it a 'grievous evil' when wealth is 'hoarded to the harm of its owner.' What questions does this raise for you about how riches could end up harming the very person who owns them?

Invite the learner's own questions; do not resolve the tension for them. Anchor in the text: v.13 names the fact (wealth 'hoarded to the harm of its owner'), and vv.14-17 show shapes it can take — a failed venture, nothing to pass on, days of sorrow. Good questions probe how a good thing turns harmful when clung to. Record their wondering; do not assess character.

For several verses the Preacher stacks up 'grievous evil' observations about riches; then verse 18 says, 'Here is what I have seen to be good and fitting.' What changes at that turn, and why does he now call eating, drinking, and finding satisfaction in one's labor 'good'?

Read the two poles together, not the lament alone. The accumulating 'grievous evil' observations (vv.10-17) pivot at v.18 into the resolution: the same riches that are futile when loved and hoarded become good when received as one's God-given lot and enjoyed. The 'why' is stated in vv.18-19 — this is a man's 'lot,' and the power to enjoy it is 'a gift from God.' Meaning is drawn from the text itself, not speculation.

The same thing — riches — is called 'futile' when a man loves and hoards it (vv.10-13) yet 'a gift from God' when he enjoys it (v.19). According to the passage, what makes the difference between riches as vanity and riches as gift?

Anchor to the text before drawing the point. The difference is not the object but its reception: loving and hoarding money never satisfies (v.10) and can harm (v.13), while receiving riches as one's God-given lot — with the God-given power to enjoy them — makes them a gift (vv.18-19). Letting Scripture interpret Scripture, the same possession is vanity or blessing depending on whether it is grasped or received from God's hand.

On an index card, draw one thing you already have that you can thank God for and enjoy today — a meal, a warm bed, someone who loves you. (Pre-reader variant: draw it, and a helper writes the word beside your picture.)

Observable action only: the child produces a card naming or drawing one present good to enjoy with thanks, echoing vv.18-19 (enjoying one's lot as God's gift). Note that a card was made and what it shows; do not assess the child's gratitude or heart.

Verse 18 calls it good to 'find satisfaction in all the labor one does.' Name one specific chore, task, or bit of schoolwork you will do this week, and one concrete way you could do it with contentment rather than wishing you were doing something else.

Looks for a concrete, doable response tied to v.18 — a named task plus one specific way to receive it as one's lot rather than resent it. Accept any real, observable plan; record what the learner names. Do not grade attitude or measure sincerity — only note the action they commit to.

The passage sets contentment with one's God-given lot against the restless love of more. Name one concrete practice this week — a purchase declined, a gift given, a meal enjoyed without hurry — by which you would receive what you have as a gift rather than chase more.

Looks for one specific, doable practice tied to the passage's contrast between hoarding (vv.10-13) and enjoying one's lot as gift (vv.18-19). Accept any concrete action; record it. Evidence, not judgment — do not evaluate the person's faith or generosity, only note the step they choose.
Listen for the word 'satisfied.' Who does the Preacher say is never satisfied?
The one who loves money and wealth: 'He who loves money is never satisfied by money, and he who loves wealth is never satisfied by income' (v.10).
Listen for the moment the Preacher stops describing trouble and says something is 'good and fitting.' What does he say is good?
To eat, drink, and find satisfaction in one's labor as God's given lot: 'Here is what I have seen to be good and fitting: to eat and drink, and to find satisfaction in all the labor one does' (v.18).
Listen through the passage for who gives riches, wealth, and even the power to enjoy them. Who is named?
God. Verse 19 says God 'has given riches and wealth to every man, and He has enabled him to enjoy them'; the ability to enjoy is itself 'a gift from God.'

In your own words, retell what the Preacher observed about riches in this passage — from the person who is never satisfied, through the man who arrives and departs with nothing, to the turn where he calls eating, drinking, and enjoying one's labor a gift from God.

A faithful retelling touches the main beats: the lover of money is never satisfied (v.10); more goods bring more consumers and lost sleep (vv.11-12); wealth can harm, be lost, or leave nothing behind (vv.13-14); a man comes and departs naked, carrying nothing (vv.15-16); and the closing turn — to eat, drink, and find satisfaction in one's labor is good, for the power to enjoy riches is a gift from God (vv.18-20). Note which beats the learner recalls; do not grade wording.
In this passage, who is it that gives a man riches, wealth, and honor?
God. Verse 2 says God gives a man 'riches, wealth, and honor, so that he lacks nothing his heart desires.'
According to verse 2, the man is not allowed to enjoy his wealth. Who does the passage say will enjoy it instead?
A stranger. Verse 2: 'Instead, a stranger will enjoy them.'
In verse 2 the Preacher calls the wealthy man's situation 'futile and a grievous affliction.' Using only what the passage says, why is this wealth called futile — what happens to it?

The wealth is futile because it goes unused by its owner: God gives the man riches yet 'does not allow him to enjoy them,' and 'a stranger will enjoy them' instead (v2). It is vain not because it is worthless but because he never has the good of it.
Verse 2 says God does not allow the man to 'enjoy' his riches. In your own words, what does it mean to 'enjoy' something?

To enjoy something is to take real pleasure or delight in it — to get gladness and good from it, not merely to own or keep it. That word carries the weight of verse 2: the man has riches but 'God does not allow him to enjoy them,' so the passage treats enjoyment itself as a God-given ability, not something wealth produces on its own.
Listen for the phrase 'under the sun.' In which verses do you hear it, and what does it seem to point to?
It appears in verse 1 and verse 12. The phrase marks the horizon of life as observed here below, in this fleeting earthly life apart from what lies beyond it.
Better what the eye can see than the wandering of desire. This too is futile and a pursuit of the wind. (Ecclesiastes 6:9)
Listen for what verse 7 says about a man's appetite. Finish the line you hear: 'yet his appetite is never ___.'
Satisfied. Verse 7: 'All a man’s labor is for his mouth, yet his appetite is never satisfied.'
Retell Ecclesiastes 6:1-12 in your own words, as if explaining to a friend the evil the Preacher saw and why he says a man's riches can be futile.
